question:
6. Find a necessary and sufficient condition on the positive integer \( n \) that the equation
\[
x^{n}+(2+x)^{n}+(2-x)^{n}=0
\]
have a rational root.
solution:
Solution. There can be no real root if \( n \) is even, since for real \( x \) each term is non-negative and they cannot vanish simultaneously.

If \( n=1 \), there is obviously a unique root \( x=-4 \).
Suppose \( n \) is odd and at least 3 . When the terms of the equation are expanded and collected, the result is monic with all coefficients nonnegative integers and constant term \( 2^{n+1} \). The only possible roots therefore are of the form \( -2^{\prime} \). For \( x=-1 \), all three terms of the given expression are odd, so -1 is not a root. Putting \( x=-2 \), we find ( -2\( )^{n}+0+ \) \( 4^{n} \neq 0 \), so -2 is not a root. If we put \( x=-2^{p+1} \) where \( p \geq 1 \), the left member of (1) becomes
\[
\begin{aligned}
2^{n}\left[-2^{p n}+\left(1-2^{p}\right)^{n}\right. & \left.+\left(1+2^{p}\right)^{n}\right] \\
& =2^{n}\left[-2^{p n}+2\left\{1+\binom{n}{2} 2^{2 p}+\binom{n}{4} 2^{4 p}+\cdots\right\}\right] .
\end{aligned}
\]

The expression in the brackets is \( \equiv 2[\bmod 4] \) (recall we are assuming \( n \geq 3 \) ), so \( -2^{p+1} \) is not a root for \( p \geq 1 \). So there are no roots if \( n>1 \). Summarizing, relation (1) has a rational root if and only if \( n=1 \).

Remark. A root with \( \boldsymbol{n}>\mathbf{2} \) would give a counterexample to the famous Fermat Conjecture.

descriptive_long.question:
6. Find a necessary and sufficient condition on the positive integer \( exponent \) that the equation
\[
variable^{exponent}+(2+variable)^{exponent}+(2-variable)^{exponent}=0
\]
have a rational root.
descriptive_long.solution:
There can be no real root if \( exponent \) is even, since for real \( variable \) each term is non-negative and they cannot vanish simultaneously.

If \( exponent=1 \), there is obviously a unique root \( variable=-4 \).
Suppose \( exponent \) is odd and at least 3. When the terms of the equation are expanded and collected, the result is monic with all coefficients nonnegative integers and constant term \( 2^{exponent+1} \). The only possible roots therefore are of the form \( -2^{\prime} \). For \( variable=-1 \), all three terms of the given expression are odd, so -1 is not a root. Putting \( variable=-2 \), we find \( (-2)^{exponent}+0+4^{exponent} \neq 0 \), so -2 is not a root. If we put \( variable=-2^{poweridx+1} \) where \( poweridx \geq 1 \), the left member of (1) becomes
\[
\begin{aligned}
2^{exponent}\left[-2^{poweridx\,exponent}+\left(1-2^{poweridx}\right)^{exponent}\right. & \left.+\left(1+2^{poweridx}\right)^{exponent}\right] \\
& =2^{exponent}\left[-2^{poweridx\,exponent}+2\left\{1+\binom{exponent}{2} 2^{2\,poweridx}+\binom{exponent}{4} 2^{4\,poweridx}+\cdots\right\}\right] .
\end{aligned}
\]

The expression in the brackets is \( \equiv 2[\bmod 4] \) (recall we are assuming \( exponent \geq 3 \) ), so \( -2^{poweridx+1} \) is not a root for \( poweridx \geq 1 \). So there are no roots if \( exponent>1 \). Summarizing, relation (1) has a rational root if and only if \( exponent=1 \).

Remark. A root with \( \boldsymbol{exponent}>\mathbf{2} \) would give a counterexample to the famous Fermat Conjecture.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
6. Find a necessary and sufficient condition on the positive integer \( starlight \) that the equation
\[
cloudburst^{starlight}+(2+cloudburst)^{starlight}+(2-cloudburst)^{starlight}=0
\]
have a rational root.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. There can be no real root if \( starlight \) is even, since for real \( cloudburst \) each term is non-negative and they cannot vanish simultaneously.

If \( starlight=1 \), there is obviously a unique root \( cloudburst=-4 \).
Suppose \( starlight \) is odd and at least 3 . When the terms of the equation are expanded and collected, the result is monic with all coefficients nonnegative integers and constant term \( 2^{starlight+1} \). The only possible roots therefore are of the form \( -2^{\prime} \). For \( cloudburst=-1 \), all three terms of the given expression are odd, so -1 is not a root. Putting \( cloudburst=-2 \), we find ( -2\( )^{starlight}+0+ \) \( 4^{starlight} \neq 0 \), so -2 is not a root. If we put \( cloudburst=-2^{driftwood+1} \) where \( driftwood \geq 1 \), the left member of (1) becomes
\[
\begin{aligned}
2^{starlight}\left[-2^{driftwood\, starlight}+\left(1-2^{driftwood}\right)^{starlight}\right. & \left.+\left(1+2^{driftwood}\right)^{starlight}\right] \\
& =2^{starlight}\left[-2^{driftwood\, starlight}+2\left\{1+\binom{starlight}{2} 2^{2 driftwood}+\binom{starlight}{4} 2^{4 driftwood}+\cdots\right\}\right] .
\end{aligned}
\]

The expression in the brackets is \( \equiv 2[\bmod 4] \) (recall we are assuming \( starlight \geq 3 \) ), so \( -2^{driftwood+1} \) is not a root for \( driftwood \geq 1 \). So there are no roots if \( starlight>1 \). Summarizing, relation (1) has a rational root if and only if \( starlight=1 \).

Remark. A root with \( \boldsymbol{starlight}>\mathbf{2} \) would give a counterexample to the famous Fermat Conjecture.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
6. Find a necessary and sufficient condition on the positive integer \( fractionalexp \) that the equation
\[
constantval^{fractionalexp}+(2+constantval)^{fractionalexp}+(2-constantval)^{fractionalexp}=0
\]
have a rational root.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. There can be no real root if \( fractionalexp \) is even, since for real \( constantval \) each term is non-negative and they cannot vanish simultaneously.

If \( fractionalexp=1 \), there is obviously a unique root \( constantval=-4 \).
Suppose \( fractionalexp \) is odd and at least 3 . When the terms of the equation are expanded and collected, the result is monic with all coefficients nonnegative integers and constant term \( 2^{fractionalexp+1} \). The only possible roots therefore are of the form \( -2^{\prime} \). For \( constantval=-1 \), all three terms of the given expression are odd, so -1 is not a root. Putting \( constantval=-2 \), we find ( -2\( )^{fractionalexp}+0+ \) \( 4^{fractionalexp} \neq 0 \), so -2 is not a root. If we put \( constantval=-2^{compositefig+1} \) where \( compositefig \geq 1 \), the left member of (1) becomes
\[
\begin{aligned}
2^{fractionalexp}\left[-2^{compositefig fractionalexp}+\left(1-2^{compositefig}\right)^{fractionalexp}\right. & \left.+\left(1+2^{compositefig}\right)^{fractionalexp}\right] \\
& =2^{fractionalexp}\left[-2^{compositefig fractionalexp}+2\left\{1+\binom{fractionalexp}{2} 2^{2 compositefig}+\binom{fractionalexp}{4} 2^{4 compositefig}+\cdots\right\}\right] .
\end{aligned}
\]

The expression in the brackets is \( \equiv 2[\bmod 4] \) (recall we are assuming \( fractionalexp \geq 3 \) ), so \( -2^{compositefig+1} \) is not a root for \( compositefig \geq 1 \). So there are no roots if \( fractionalexp>1 \). Summarizing, relation (1) has a rational root if and only if \( fractionalexp=1 \).

Remark. A root with \( \boldsymbol{fractionalexp}>\mathbf{2} \) would give a counterexample to the famous Fermat Conjecture.

garbled_string.question:
Find a necessary and sufficient condition on the positive integer \( vbmqcrdu \) that the equation
\[
qzxwvtnp^{vbmqcrdu}+(2+qzxwvtnp)^{vbmqcrdu}+(2-qzxwvtnp)^{vbmqcrdu}=0
\]
have a rational root.
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. There can be no real root if \( vbmqcrdu \) is even, since for real \( qzxwvtnp \) each term is non-negative and they cannot vanish simultaneously.

If \( vbmqcrdu=1 \), there is obviously a unique root \( qzxwvtnp=-4 \).
Suppose \( vbmqcrdu \) is odd and at least 3 . When the terms of the equation are expanded and collected, the result is monic with all coefficients nonnegative integers and constant term \( 2^{vbmqcrdu+1} \). The only possible roots therefore are of the form \( -2^{\prime} \). For \( qzxwvtnp=-1 \), all three terms of the given expression are odd, so -1 is not a root. Putting \( qzxwvtnp=-2 \), we find ( -2\( )^{vbmqcrdu}+0+ \) \( 4^{vbmqcrdu} \neq 0 \), so -2 is not a root. If we put \( qzxwvtnp=-2^{hjgrksla+1} \) where \( hjgrksla \geq 1 \), the left member of (1) becomes
\[
\begin{aligned}
2^{vbmqcrdu}\left[-2^{hjgrksla vbmqcrdu}+\left(1-2^{hjgrksla}\right)^{vbmqcrdu}\right. & \left.+\left(1+2^{hjgrksla}\right)^{vbmqcrdu}\right] \\
& =2^{vbmqcrdu}\left[-2^{hjgrksla vbmqcrdu}+2\left\{1+\binom{vbmqcrdu}{2} 2^{2 hjgrksla}+\binom{vbmqcrdu}{4} 2^{4 hjgrksla}+\cdots\right\}\right] .
\end{aligned}
\]

The expression in the brackets is \( \equiv 2[\bmod 4] \) (recall we are assuming \( vbmqcrdu \geq 3 \) ), so \( -2^{hjgrksla+1} \) is not a root for \( hjgrksla \geq 1 \). So there are no roots if \( vbmqcrdu>1 \). Summarizing, relation (1) has a rational root if and only if \( vbmqcrdu=1 \).

Remark. A root with \( \boldsymbol{vbmqcrdu}>\mathbf{2} \) would give a counterexample to the famous Fermat Conjecture.

kernel_variant.question:
Fix the integer  
\[
d=2
\]  
and, for every positive integer \(n\), define  
\[
P_{n}(y)=\bigl(y-2\bigr)^{n}+y^{\,n}+\bigl(y+2\bigr)^{n}\in\mathbf{Q}[y]\qquad(n\ge 1).
\]

1.  For two \emph{distinct} positive integers \(m<n\), determine explicitly the monic polynomial  
   \[
      \gcd_{\mathbf{Q}[y]}\bigl(P_{m},P_{n}\bigr).
   \]

2.  Find all ordered pairs \((m,n)\) of distinct positive integers for which \(P_{m}\) and \(P_{n}\) possess a common rational root, and list every such root.

3.  Classify (with proof) all non-empty finite index sets  
   \[
      \mathcal{N}\subset\mathbf{N}
   \]
   for which there exists \(r\in\mathbf{Q}\) satisfying \(P_{n}(r)=0\) for \emph{every} \(n\in\mathcal{N}\).

(The three questions are strongly coupled; answers obtained independently must nevertheless be proved within a coherent global argument.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we let  
\[
P_{n}(y)=\bigl(y-2\bigr)^{n}+y^{\,n}+\bigl(y+2\bigr)^{n}\qquad(n\in\mathbf{N})
\]
and we always choose a greatest common divisor to be monic.

1.  A preliminary analysis of a single polynomial \(P_{n}\)

(a)  Parity.  
\[
P_{n}(-y)=(-1)^{\,n}P_{n}(y)\qquad(n\ge 1),
\]
so \(P_{n}\) is even when \(n\) is even and odd when \(n\) is odd.

(b)  A factorisation when \(n\) is odd.  
Put \(n=2k+1\) (\(k\ge 0\)).  Expanding \((y\pm2)^{2k+1}\) gives  
\[
\begin{aligned}
P_{n}(y)
      &=(y-2)^{2k+1}+y^{2k+1}+(y+2)^{2k+1}\\
      &=y\!\Bigl[\,3y^{2k}
        +2\sum_{j=1}^{k}\binom{2k+1}{2j}2^{2j}y^{2(k-j)}\Bigr]
      =:y\,Q_{n}(y),
\end{aligned}
\]
where \(Q_{n}\in\mathbf{Z}[y]\) has only positive coefficients; hence  
\[
Q_{n}(y)>0\quad\forall\,y\in\mathbf{R}. \tag{1.1}
\]

(c)  Real and rational roots.

Lemma 1.1.  
If \(n\) is even, then \(P_{n}(y)>0\) for all \(y\in\mathbf{R}\); consequently \(P_{n}\) has no rational root.

Proof.  Each of the three summands is non-negative, and at most one of them can vanish; hence the sum is strictly positive. \(\square\)

Lemma 1.2.  
If \(n\) is odd, the only rational root of \(P_{n}\) is \(y=0\).  This root is simple and  
\[
P_{n}'(0)=
\begin{cases}
3,&n=1,\\[4pt]
n\,2^{\,n},&n\ge 3\text{ odd}.
\end{cases}
\]

Proof.  By (1.1) we have \(P_{n}(y)=y\,Q_{n}(y)\) with \(Q_{n}(y)>0\) on \(\mathbf{R}\), so the sole real (and hence rational) zero is \(0\).  Differentiating and evaluating at \(0\) gives the claimed formula; for \(n=1\) the value is \(3\). \(\square\)

Corollary 1.3.  
\(P_{n}\) has a rational root exactly when \(n\) is odd; then the unique rational root is \(y=0\), which is simple.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Uniqueness of a non-zero common root  

Fix a non-zero complex number \(z\) and set  
\[
u:=\frac{z-2}{z},\qquad v:=\frac{z+2}{z}=2-u,\qquad
c:=uv=1-\frac{4}{z^{2}}\neq 0.
\]
Because \(z\neq 0\),  
\[
P_{n}(z)=0\;\Longleftrightarrow\;S_{n}:=u^{\,n}+v^{\,n}+1=0. \tag{2.1}
\]
The sequence \(\bigl(S_{n}\bigr)_{n\ge 0}\) satisfies the inhomogeneous linear recurrence  
\[
S_{n+2}-2\,S_{n+1}+c\,S_{n}=c-1\qquad(n\ge 0). \tag{2.2}
\]

Proposition 2.1 (Uniqueness).  
For a fixed non-zero complex number \(z\) the equality \(P_{n}(z)=0\) can hold for \emph{at most one} positive integer \(n\).

Proof.  
Assume, by contradiction, that \(P_{m}(z)=P_{n}(z)=0\) for some integers \(1\le m<n\); put \(d:=n-m\ge 1\).

Step 1.  A basic identity.  
Using \(S_{m}=S_{n}=0\) we derive
\[
u^{m}\bigl(u^{d}-1\bigr)=-v^{m}\bigl(v^{d}-1\bigr). \tag{2.3}
\]

Step 2.  The case \(\lvert u\rvert\neq\lvert v\rvert\).  
Set \(r:=\lvert u/v\rvert\neq 1\).  Taking absolute values in (2.3) yields
\[
r^{\,m}=
\frac{\lvert v^{d}-1\rvert}{\lvert u^{d}-1\rvert}=:\kappa(d;u,v),
\]
where \(\kappa(d;u,v)\) is independent of \(m\).  Because \(r\neq 1\), the left-hand side is a strictly monotone function of \(m\); hence equality is impossible for two distinct integers \(m\).  Thus the simultaneous vanishing of \(S_{m}\) and \(S_{n}\) cannot occur.

Step 3.  The critical case \(\lvert u\rvert=\lvert v\rvert\).  
Write  
\[
u=r\,e^{\,i\theta},\quad v=r\,e^{-i\theta},\qquad r>1,\ 0<\theta<\pi, \tag{2.4}
\]
since \(u+v=2\) forces \(r\cos\theta=1\) and therefore \(r>1\).  
With (2.4) we have  
\[
S_{k}=2\,r^{k}\cos(k\theta)+1. \tag{2.5}
\]

Suppose \(S_{m}=S_{n}=0\) with \(m<n\).  Then (2.5) gives  
\[
\cos(m\theta)=-\frac{1}{2r^{m}},\qquad
\cos(n\theta)=-\frac{1}{2r^{n}}. \tag{2.6}
\]

Returning to (2.3) and using \(v/u=e^{-2i\theta}\), we obtain  
\[
u^{d}-1=-e^{-2im\theta}\,\overline{u^{d}-1}. \tag{2.7}
\]
Write \(A:=u^{d}-1\).  Since \(r>1\), \(u^{d}\neq 1\) and \(A\neq 0\).  
Taking complex conjugates in (2.7) and multiplying the two equalities gives  
\[
\bigl(1-e^{-2im\theta}\bigr)\,|A|^{2}=0.
\]
Hence \(e^{-2im\theta}=1\); that is,  
\[
2m\theta=2\pi k\quad(k\in\mathbf{Z}). \tag{2.8}
\]

Consequently \(\cos(m\theta)=1\).  Plugging this into the first relation in (2.6) yields  
\[
1=-\frac{1}{2r^{m}},
\]
contradicting \(r>1\).  Therefore the assumption \(S_{m}=S_{n}=0\) is impossible.

Combining Steps 2 and 3 completes the proof. \(\square\)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  The greatest common divisor  

Proposition 3.1.  For \(m<n\) we have  
\[
\gcd_{\mathbf{Q}[y]}\bigl(P_{m},P_{n}\bigr)=
\begin{cases}
y,&\text{if }m,n\text{ are both odd},\\[6pt]
1,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

Proof.  

(i)  Assume at least one exponent, say \(m\), is even.  
Then \(P_{m}\) possesses no rational root (Lemma 1.1), so \(y\nmid P_{m}\).  
Suppose, towards a contradiction, that a non-constant \(D\in\mathbf{Q}[y]\) divides both \(P_{m}\) and \(P_{n}\).  Let \(z\) be a root of \(D\).  

* If \(z=0\) then \(P_{m}(0)=2^{\,m}\neq 0\), impossible.  

* If \(z\neq 0\) then \(P_{m}(z)=P_{n}(z)=0\), contradicting Proposition 2.1.  

Hence \(\gcd=1\).

(ii)  Now suppose both exponents are odd.  
By Corollary 1.3, \(y\mid P_{m}\) and \(y\mid P_{n}\), so \(y\) divides the gcd.  
The zero at \(y=0\) is simple in each polynomial (Lemma 1.2), implying \(y^{2}\nmid P_{m}\) and therefore \(y^{2}\nmid\gcd\).  
If the gcd possessed another irreducible factor, it would yield a non-zero common root, impossible by Proposition 2.1.  Thus \(\gcd=y\).  \(\square\)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Common rational roots of two members  

Theorem 4.1.  
The polynomials \(P_{m}\) and \(P_{n}\) share a rational root precisely when both \(m\) and \(n\) are odd; their only common rational root is \(y=0\).

Proof.  
If one exponent is even, the corresponding polynomial has no rational root (Lemma 1.1); hence no common rational root exists.  
If both exponents are odd, Corollary 1.3 gives the common root \(0\), and Proposition 3.1 shows there is no other.  \(\square\)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Simultaneous vanishing on a finite set of indices  

Let \(\varnothing\neq\mathcal{N}\subset\mathbf{N}\) be finite.  
By Corollary 1.3, \(P_{n}\) has a rational root iff \(n\) is odd, in which case the only rational root is \(0\).  Consequently  
\[
\exists\,r\in\mathbf{Q}\;
   \forall\,n\in\mathcal{N}:P_{n}(r)=0
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\mathcal{N}\subset 2\mathbf{N}-1,
\]
and then necessarily \(r=0\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Answer summary  

1.  \(\displaystyle
       \gcd_{\mathbf{Q}[y]}\bigl(P_{m},P_{n}\bigr)=
       \begin{cases}
         y,&\text{if }m,n\text{ are odd},\\
         1,&\text{otherwise}.
       \end{cases}\)

2.  \(P_{m}\) and \(P_{n}\) possess a common rational root iff both indices are odd; that root is \(y=0\) (simple in each polynomial).

3.  A finite non-empty set \(\mathcal{N}\subset\mathbf{N}\) admits a rational \(r\) with \(P_{n}(r)=0\) for all \(n\in\mathcal{N}\) precisely when every element of \(\mathcal{N}\) is odd; then \(r=0\) is forced.

\[
\square
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
Compared with the original single-equation question, the enhanced
variant demands:

• Working simultaneously with two (and in part 3, arbitrarily many)
different exponents, so one must analyse a whole family of polynomials
instead of a single one.

• Determining the exact greatest common divisor of two high-degree
trinomials in ℚ[y].  This obliges the solver to mix structural
parity arguments with polynomial factorisation over ℚ—concepts that do
not appear in the original exercise.

• Proving coprimality of quotient polynomials, which requires a
non-trivial combination of the Rational Root Theorem, parity
considerations, modular arithmetic and multiplicity arguments.

• Classifying all finite index sets that admit a common rational root,
which converts the problem from an isolated Diophantine question into
a global description invoking results from the previous parts.

Each of these layers adds substantial algebraic complexity and forces
the use of several advanced tools (factorisation in polynomial rings,
gcd computations, root multiplicities, and parity-based modular
obstructions), making the enhanced variant markedly more intricate
than both the original problem and the current kernel version.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Fix the integer  
\[
d=2
\]  
and, for every positive integer \(n\), define  
\[
P_{n}(y)=\bigl(y-2\bigr)^{n}+y^{\,n}+\bigl(y+2\bigr)^{n}\in\mathbf{Q}[y]\qquad(n\ge 1).
\]

1.  For two \emph{distinct} positive integers \(m<n\), determine explicitly the monic polynomial  
   \[
      \gcd_{\mathbf{Q}[y]}\bigl(P_{m},P_{n}\bigr).
   \]

2.  Find all ordered pairs \((m,n)\) of distinct positive integers for which \(P_{m}\) and \(P_{n}\) possess a common rational root, and list every such root.

3.  Classify (with proof) all non-empty finite index sets  
   \[
      \mathcal{N}\subset\mathbf{N}
   \]
   for which there exists \(r\in\mathbf{Q}\) satisfying \(P_{n}(r)=0\) for \emph{every} \(n\in\mathcal{N}\).

(The three questions are strongly coupled; answers obtained independently must nevertheless be proved within a coherent global argument.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we let  
\[
P_{n}(y)=\bigl(y-2\bigr)^{n}+y^{\,n}+\bigl(y+2\bigr)^{n}\qquad(n\in\mathbf{N})
\]
and we always choose a greatest common divisor to be monic.

1.  A preliminary analysis of a single polynomial \(P_{n}\)

(a)  Parity.  
\[
P_{n}(-y)=(-1)^{\,n}P_{n}(y)\qquad(n\ge 1),
\]
so \(P_{n}\) is even when \(n\) is even and odd when \(n\) is odd.

(b)  A factorisation when \(n\) is odd.  
Put \(n=2k+1\) (\(k\ge 0\)).  Expanding \((y\pm2)^{2k+1}\) gives  
\[
\begin{aligned}
P_{n}(y)
      &=(y-2)^{2k+1}+y^{2k+1}+(y+2)^{2k+1}\\
      &=y\!\Bigl[\,3y^{2k}
        +2\sum_{j=1}^{k}\binom{2k+1}{2j}2^{2j}y^{2(k-j)}\Bigr]
      =:y\,Q_{n}(y),
\end{aligned}
\]
where \(Q_{n}\in\mathbf{Z}[y]\) has only positive coefficients; hence  
\[
Q_{n}(y)>0\quad\forall\,y\in\mathbf{R}. \tag{1.1}
\]

(c)  Real and rational roots.

Lemma 1.1.  
If \(n\) is even, then \(P_{n}(y)>0\) for all \(y\in\mathbf{R}\); consequently \(P_{n}\) has no rational root.

Proof.  Each of the three summands is non-negative, and at most one of them can vanish; hence the sum is strictly positive. \(\square\)

Lemma 1.2.  
If \(n\) is odd, the only rational root of \(P_{n}\) is \(y=0\).  This root is simple and  
\[
P_{n}'(0)=
\begin{cases}
3,&n=1,\\[4pt]
n\,2^{\,n},&n\ge 3\text{ odd}.
\end{cases}
\]

Proof.  By (1.1) we have \(P_{n}(y)=y\,Q_{n}(y)\) with \(Q_{n}(y)>0\) on \(\mathbf{R}\), so the sole real (and hence rational) zero is \(0\).  Differentiating and evaluating at \(0\) gives the claimed formula; for \(n=1\) the value is \(3\). \(\square\)

Corollary 1.3.  
\(P_{n}\) has a rational root exactly when \(n\) is odd; then the unique rational root is \(y=0\), which is simple.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Uniqueness of a non-zero common root  

Fix a non-zero complex number \(z\) and set  
\[
u:=\frac{z-2}{z},\qquad v:=\frac{z+2}{z}=2-u,\qquad
c:=uv=1-\frac{4}{z^{2}}\neq 0.
\]
Because \(z\neq 0\),  
\[
P_{n}(z)=0\;\Longleftrightarrow\;S_{n}:=u^{\,n}+v^{\,n}+1=0. \tag{2.1}
\]
The sequence \(\bigl(S_{n}\bigr)_{n\ge 0}\) satisfies the inhomogeneous linear recurrence  
\[
S_{n+2}-2\,S_{n+1}+c\,S_{n}=c-1\qquad(n\ge 0). \tag{2.2}
\]

Proposition 2.1 (Uniqueness).  
For a fixed non-zero complex number \(z\) the equality \(P_{n}(z)=0\) can hold for \emph{at most one} positive integer \(n\).

Proof.  
Assume, by contradiction, that \(P_{m}(z)=P_{n}(z)=0\) for some integers \(1\le m<n\); put \(d:=n-m\ge 1\).

Step 1.  A basic identity.  
Using \(S_{m}=S_{n}=0\) we derive
\[
u^{m}\bigl(u^{d}-1\bigr)=-v^{m}\bigl(v^{d}-1\bigr). \tag{2.3}
\]

Step 2.  The case \(\lvert u\rvert\neq\lvert v\rvert\).  
Set \(r:=\lvert u/v\rvert\neq 1\).  Taking absolute values in (2.3) yields
\[
r^{\,m}=
\frac{\lvert v^{d}-1\rvert}{\lvert u^{d}-1\rvert}=:\kappa(d;u,v),
\]
where \(\kappa(d;u,v)\) is independent of \(m\).  Because \(r\neq 1\), the left-hand side is a strictly monotone function of \(m\); hence equality is impossible for two distinct integers \(m\).  Thus the simultaneous vanishing of \(S_{m}\) and \(S_{n}\) cannot occur.

Step 3.  The critical case \(\lvert u\rvert=\lvert v\rvert\).  
Write  
\[
u=r\,e^{\,i\theta},\quad v=r\,e^{-i\theta},\qquad r>1,\ 0<\theta<\pi, \tag{2.4}
\]
since \(u+v=2\) forces \(r\cos\theta=1\) and therefore \(r>1\).  
With (2.4) we have  
\[
S_{k}=2\,r^{k}\cos(k\theta)+1. \tag{2.5}
\]

Suppose \(S_{m}=S_{n}=0\) with \(m<n\).  Then (2.5) gives  
\[
\cos(m\theta)=-\frac{1}{2r^{m}},\qquad
\cos(n\theta)=-\frac{1}{2r^{n}}. \tag{2.6}
\]

Returning to (2.3) and using \(v/u=e^{-2i\theta}\), we obtain  
\[
u^{d}-1=-e^{-2im\theta}\,\overline{u^{d}-1}. \tag{2.7}
\]
Write \(A:=u^{d}-1\).  Since \(r>1\), \(u^{d}\neq 1\) and \(A\neq 0\).  
Taking complex conjugates in (2.7) and multiplying the two equalities gives  
\[
\bigl(1-e^{-2im\theta}\bigr)\,|A|^{2}=0.
\]
Hence \(e^{-2im\theta}=1\); that is,  
\[
2m\theta=2\pi k\quad(k\in\mathbf{Z}). \tag{2.8}
\]

Consequently \(\cos(m\theta)=1\).  Plugging this into the first relation in (2.6) yields  
\[
1=-\frac{1}{2r^{m}},
\]
contradicting \(r>1\).  Therefore the assumption \(S_{m}=S_{n}=0\) is impossible.

Combining Steps 2 and 3 completes the proof. \(\square\)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  The greatest common divisor  

Proposition 3.1.  For \(m<n\) we have  
\[
\gcd_{\mathbf{Q}[y]}\bigl(P_{m},P_{n}\bigr)=
\begin{cases}
y,&\text{if }m,n\text{ are both odd},\\[6pt]
1,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

Proof.  

(i)  Assume at least one exponent, say \(m\), is even.  
Then \(P_{m}\) possesses no rational root (Lemma 1.1), so \(y\nmid P_{m}\).  
Suppose, towards a contradiction, that a non-constant \(D\in\mathbf{Q}[y]\) divides both \(P_{m}\) and \(P_{n}\).  Let \(z\) be a root of \(D\).  

* If \(z=0\) then \(P_{m}(0)=2^{\,m}\neq 0\), impossible.  

* If \(z\neq 0\) then \(P_{m}(z)=P_{n}(z)=0\), contradicting Proposition 2.1.  

Hence \(\gcd=1\).

(ii)  Now suppose both exponents are odd.  
By Corollary 1.3, \(y\mid P_{m}\) and \(y\mid P_{n}\), so \(y\) divides the gcd.  
The zero at \(y=0\) is simple in each polynomial (Lemma 1.2), implying \(y^{2}\nmid P_{m}\) and therefore \(y^{2}\nmid\gcd\).  
If the gcd possessed another irreducible factor, it would yield a non-zero common root, impossible by Proposition 2.1.  Thus \(\gcd=y\).  \(\square\)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Common rational roots of two members  

Theorem 4.1.  
The polynomials \(P_{m}\) and \(P_{n}\) share a rational root precisely when both \(m\) and \(n\) are odd; their only common rational root is \(y=0\).

Proof.  
If one exponent is even, the corresponding polynomial has no rational root (Lemma 1.1); hence no common rational root exists.  
If both exponents are odd, Corollary 1.3 gives the common root \(0\), and Proposition 3.1 shows there is no other.  \(\square\)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Simultaneous vanishing on a finite set of indices  

Let \(\varnothing\neq\mathcal{N}\subset\mathbf{N}\) be finite.  
By Corollary 1.3, \(P_{n}\) has a rational root iff \(n\) is odd, in which case the only rational root is \(0\).  Consequently  
\[
\exists\,r\in\mathbf{Q}\;
   \forall\,n\in\mathcal{N}:P_{n}(r)=0
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\mathcal{N}\subset 2\mathbf{N}-1,
\]
and then necessarily \(r=0\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Answer summary  

1.  \(\displaystyle
       \gcd_{\mathbf{Q}[y]}\bigl(P_{m},P_{n}\bigr)=
       \begin{cases}
         y,&\text{if }m,n\text{ are odd},\\
         1,&\text{otherwise}.
       \end{cases}\)

2.  \(P_{m}\) and \(P_{n}\) possess a common rational root iff both indices are odd; that root is \(y=0\) (simple in each polynomial).

3.  A finite non-empty set \(\mathcal{N}\subset\mathbf{N}\) admits a rational \(r\) with \(P_{n}(r)=0\) for all \(n\in\mathcal{N}\) precisely when every element of \(\mathcal{N}\) is odd; then \(r=0\) is forced.

\[
\square
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
Compared with the original single-equation question, the enhanced
variant demands:

• Working simultaneously with two (and in part 3, arbitrarily many)
different exponents, so one must analyse a whole family of polynomials
instead of a single one.

• Determining the exact greatest common divisor of two high-degree
trinomials in ℚ[y].  This obliges the solver to mix structural
parity arguments with polynomial factorisation over ℚ—concepts that do
not appear in the original exercise.

• Proving coprimality of quotient polynomials, which requires a
non-trivial combination of the Rational Root Theorem, parity
considerations, modular arithmetic and multiplicity arguments.

• Classifying all finite index sets that admit a common rational root,
which converts the problem from an isolated Diophantine question into
a global description invoking results from the previous parts.

Each of these layers adds substantial algebraic complexity and forces
the use of several advanced tools (factorisation in polynomial rings,
gcd computations, root multiplicities, and parity-based modular
obstructions), making the enhanced variant markedly more intricate
than both the original problem and the current kernel version.
